transcript_segments.text: Welcome to the topic of requirements analysis, which is a crucial activity in the analysis phase of the system's development life circle.
As noted in the previous lecture, planning is more about generating an idea and evaluating the likelihood of implementing that idea.
As a result, we submit a system request to present the idea.
If the idea is approved by the committee, we will then conduct a feasibility analysis.
From now on, we're going to move to the analysis phase.
So what is analysis?
Simply put, analysis defines what the system is or define the requirements of a system.
Analysis will be the focus of our learning for two main reasons.
First, requirements defined at the analysis phase will serve as a crucial input for technical personnel to design and develop the system in the later stages of the SDLC.
And the second requirements definition turns out to be the most difficult and challenging face for most of the projects.
Therefore, it is important to make sure the system is defined properly before we actually build it.
Regarding today's agenda, we will start with introducing what we mean by requirements.
Then we'll focus on learning the two main types of requirements, functional and non-functional requirements.
So what do we mean by defining requirements for a system?

What we mean is to describe the functions and characteristics of a system from a business user's perspective, not from a system developer's perspective, that has a focus on the technical details of a system.
And there are two types of requirements, functional requirements that describe the functions or features of a system and non-functional requirements that describe the characteristics of the system.
Now, let's move to the focus of today's topic, that is functional and nonfunctional requirements.
A system should have functional requirements to help fulfill its business objectives.
As noted earlier, functional requirements emphasize business users and their objectives achieved with the help of a system.
Therefore, we can describe functional requirements to highlight how a system allows its business users to achieve their objectives.
As an example, if the functional requirements listed here represent our student Information System or SIS.
We could describe one of them as allow students to enroll in courses.
In this case, student is the business user, and the objective that needs to be achieved with the help of SIS is course enrollment.
Another functional requirement could be allow professors to view class roster.
In this case, professor is a business user, and the objective is to view class roster.
Oftentimes, functional requirements can be further elaborated to better describe how business objectives can be achieved.
In the example of SIS.
One functional requirement is to allow students to enroll in courses.
Before registering for courses, students may want to find out more information about the courses, such as course contents, objectives, instructors who are going to teach the courses.
Number of credits, and many more.
And this requires SIS to have a process that allows students to search for course information and view it.
Once students decide which courses to enroll.
SIS should have another process students could go through to register for courses.
In addition, There should be a different process that allows students to cancel a registered course before certain deadline.
As we can see, we can better describe the functional requirements of allow students to enroll in courses by providing more details.
Given our experience in using SIS.
I'm sure it won't be very difficult for us to map a specific function requirement to the actual implementation of the system.
As an example, Let me show you how SIS will allow students to search for course information.
To search for cost information in SIS we first need to log in.
After looking, we should see the screen below.
Then we need to choose a type of search for classes.
And then it will allow us to enter such criteria, such as the term of course is offered, the cost subject, and the cost number.
After we enter the search criteria.
We click the search button.
And the system will retrieve the cost information that matches our search criteria.
Please note that here I skip the screen capture that displays the search results.
Here we see is a process or a series of steps, the system will go through to allow students to search and view course information.
And when we go through these steps, some data or information has been processed, including your username and password for login and some course related information.
Now, let's move on to non-functional requirements.
Different from functional requirements, which describe what a system should do.
Non-functional requirements describe how well the system perform, or we can say that nonfunctional requirements represent the behavioral properties or characteristics of an information system.
There are 4 dimensions of non-functional requirements, that is.
Operational Performance Security and cultural and political.
In designing your system, you may want to make it more attractive or appealing to users.
You can do so by enhancing the 4 dimensions of a system's non-functional requirements.
Now we're going to describe each dimension in detail.
The first dimension is operational requirement.
Operational requirements address issues related to the physical and technical environments in which the system will operate.
Physical environment describes the physical attributes of a system or how well the system is designed for the physical environment.
An example that shows the physical attributes could be size, color, and sense of touch of an iPhone.
On the other hand, Technical environment that describes the capability of a system with other systems, or whether the system is able to integrate with other systems.
An example would be, iPhone could exchange GPS location with iWatch.
The 2nd dimension is performance requirement.
Performance requirements describe how well the system fulfills its intended purposes.
There could be various performance indicators, and here at least a few.
And I will explain them with some examples.
The first performance indicator is speed, and users always prefer a system that can work faster.
An example would be any interaction between the user and the system should not exist 2 seconds.
Capacity usually refers to storage capacity of memory or hard disk, and the larger capacity is generally preferred, as you can expect.
If the system is easy to use, it requires minimal effort from the user's side.
An example that makes it easier for the user is to allow the user to select from a list instead of key in the information.
Reliability refers to the system ability.
To consistently generate the correct output.
An example would be the E-channel.
When you think of the e-Chan devices at any border control point, they probably need to run for 24/7, close to 0% downtime.
That is, it consistently generates the correct output.
And there could be other indicators, and we will not list all of them here.

The 3rd dimension is security requirement, which describes the protection of the system from theft or damage to their hardware, software or information, as well as from disruption or misdirection of the services they provide.
Some example of a security control would be.
Protection of private information or confidential information.
System access is granted to only those authorized users or systems of protection from virus attack.
The last dimension is cultural and political requirement that describes the system's ability to account for cultural differences, or its vigilance to legal issues.
For example, Best Buy will customize the display of their websites based on the countries you select.
Another example will be the use of medical systems should be compliant with the rules and standards in the medical industry.

OK, after knowing the difference between functional requirements and non-functional requirements, as well as various types of non-functional requirements, it's time to test your understanding with a concept checking question.
Feel free to pause the video for a moment to work on the question before proceeding to the next slide where the solution will be provided.
Here is a solution.
Now, as a quick summary of today's topic.
We mainly cover the requirement definition that includes the description of functional requirements, that is what the system can do to allow business users to achieve certain business objectives.
And non-functional requirements, that is how well the system can perform or the behavioral properties or characteristics of a system.

As noted in the previous lecture, planning is more about generating an idea and evaluating the likelihood of implementing that idea. As a result, we submit a system request to present the idea. If the idea is approved by the committee, we will then conduct a feasibility analysis. From now on, we're going to move to the analysis phase. So what is analysis? Therefore, it is important to make sure the system is defined properly before we actually build it.
key_points: Requirements analysis is a crucial activity in the analysis phase of SDLC
Analysis defines system requirements from a business user's perspective
Proper requirements definition is critical to avoid project challenges
Two main types of requirements: functional and non-functional

concepts.name: Understanding Non-functional Requirements
concepts.description: Define non-functional requirements as system characteristics that describe performance and behavior

concepts.name: Operational Requirements
concepts.description: Explain operational requirements related to physical and technical system environments

concepts.name: Performance Requirements
concepts.description: Detail performance indicators like speed, capacity, ease of use, and reliability

concepts.name: Security Requirements
concepts.description: Describe security measures to protect system data and prevent unauthorized access

concepts.name: Cultural and Political Requirements
concepts.description: Explain requirements addressing legal compliance and cultural considerations

reel_scripts.topic: Requirements Analysis Overview > What is Requirements Analysis
reel_scripts.hook: Did you know the hardest part of building a system isn't coding? It's figuring out what the system even needs to do
reel_scripts.narration_text: Requirements analysis is the phase where you figure out what the system needs to do before you even write a line of code. It happens during the SDLC analysis stage after feasibility is confirmed. This is where you ask business users what they need the system to accomplish. Think of it like planning a road trip – you don’t start driving until you know your destination. You need to capture both functional requirements like 'add a membership' and non-functional ones like 'response time must be under two seconds'. If you skip this step, you risk building the wrong thing. That’s why requirements analysis is the most challenging phase – you’re basically translating business needs into technical specifications without any guarantees.

visual_directions.description: Show SDLC phases with analysis highlighted
Display requirements definition slide with functional/non-functional split
Show slide with example requirements from slide 1
Display summary slide with requirements types

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Requirements analysis is the process of defining what a system needs to do before building it, ensuring everyone agrees on the goals
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What are the two main types of requirements in systems development?
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: Requirements Analysis basics: Why defining system needs is harder than coding 🧠 #SDLC #SoftwareDevelopment #RequirementsAnalysis #TechTips

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your favorite apps have hidden rules that make them work? Let me explain functional requirements!
reel_scripts.narration_text: Functional requirements are the core features of a system that help people actually use it. Think of your student information system – it's not just about storing data, it's about letting students enroll in classes, professors check rosters, and everyone navigate the system smoothly. These requirements are all about what the system does for users, not how it's built. For example, allowing students to search for courses isn't just a feature – it's a process that needs detailed steps like logging in, selecting tabs, and clicking buttons. The key is focusing on business goals: if a system doesn't help users achieve their tasks, it's not functional. It's about mapping every action a user needs to accomplish and making sure the system supports it without getting lost in technical jargon.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 3 with SDLC phases
Display slide 8 with SIS functional requirements
Show slide 9 with process steps

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Functional requirements are the system's features that directly enable users to accomplish their tasks.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What do functional requirements focus on? A) Technical specifications B) User objectives C) System speed D) Developer tools
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: Functional requirements = system features that help users get things done 🎯 #SoftwareDevelopment #RequirementsAnalysis #SIS

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your app's speed and security aren't just features—they're requirements?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Non-functional requirements are the hidden rules that make systems work smoothly. Unlike features, they describe how well a system performs. Think of them as the four pillars: operational (how it fits with tech environments), performance (speed and reliability), security (protecting data), and cultural/political (legal and societal factors). These requirements ensure your system isn't just functional—it's efficient, safe, and ready for the real world.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 10 with the four non-functional dimensions
Show slide 11 explaining operational requirements
Show slide 12 with performance indicators like speed and reliability
Show slide 13 highlighting security examples
Show slide 14 on cultural/political requirements

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Non-functional requirements ensure your system works well, stays secure, and fits the real world.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: Which of these is a non-functional requirement?
quiz_choices: A) Allow users to reset passwords
B) Ensure the system handles 10,000 users at once
C) Let admins manage user roles
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: What makes a system great? 🚀 Non-functional requirements ensure it's fast, secure, and fits the real world! #TechTips #SoftwareDev #SystemDesign

reel_scripts.topic: Requirements Analysis Overview > Role of Analysis in SDLC
reel_scripts.hook: Did you know the first step in building a system is actually defining what it needs to do?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Requirements analysis is the bridge between what users want and how developers build it. During this phase, we translate business needs into clear requirements that guide every part of the system design and development. These requirements act as a roadmap for engineers, ensuring everyone stays aligned. If you get this wrong, you're basically building a house without blueprints. That's why analysis is the most critical phase—it defines the system's soul before any code is written.

visual_directions.description: Show SDLC phases with analysis highlighted
Display slide emphasizing analysis as input for design

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Requirements analysis is the foundation that guides every step of the SDLC, ensuring the system is built exactly as needed.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What's the main purpose of requirements analysis in SDLC?
quiz_choices: A) Defining system requirements for design and development
B) Writing code for the system
D) Marketing the system to users
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: 🔍 Requirements analysis is the blueprint for your system! #SDLC #SoftwareDevelopment #ProjectManagement #IT

reel_scripts.topic: Functional Requirements > Examples of Functional Requirements
reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your student system has hidden rules that let students enroll in classes? Let's break them down.
reel_scripts.narration_text: Functional requirements are the core features of a system from the user's perspective. Take a student information system—examples include letting students enroll in courses, search for class details, or cancel registrations. Professors also need to view class rosters. These requirements aren't just features—they're the specific processes that help users achieve their goals. For instance, the enrollment process might involve searching course info, selecting classes, and confirming registration. Each step is a functional requirement that ensures the system works exactly how users need it to.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 8 with functional requirements list
Show slide 9 with process steps

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Functional requirements are the specific features that let users accomplish tasks like course enrollment or viewing rosters.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: Which of these is an example of a functional requirement?
quiz_choices: A) System must handle 1000 users at once
B) Allow professors to view class rosters
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: Functional requirements = user tasks made possible by a system 💡 #SoftwareRequirements #SISExamples #SystemDesign

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your phone's design and how it connects to other devices matters more than you think?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Operational requirements are all about making sure a system works smoothly in its physical and technical environment. Think of it like preparing a recipe—you need the right ingredients and tools. For physical environment, it's about things like size, color, and how it feels in your hands. For technical environment, it's about compatibility with other systems. Like how your iPhone works with Apple Watch or how a school system needs to integrate with other software. These requirements ensure the system doesn't just function, but functions reliably in the real world.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 10 with non-functional requirements overview
Show slide 11 detailing operational requirements

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Operational requirements ensure a system works reliably in its physical environment and technical ecosystem.
quiz_choices: A) Physical attributes and technical compatibility
B) Security protocols and data encryption
C) User interface design and aesthetics
D) Legal compliance and cultural standards
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: Operational Requirements: Why your system's environment matters 📌 #SoftwareDesign #SystemRequirements #TechTips

reel_scripts.topic: Requirements Analysis Overview > Importance of Proper Requirements Definition
reel_scripts.hook: What if I told you 70% of projects fail because they start building the wrong thing?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Requirements analysis is the foundation of any project. If you skip this step, you're basically building a house without blueprints. Imagine spending months coding only to realize the system doesn't actually solve the problem. That's why defining requirements upfront is critical — it aligns everyone's expectations and prevents costly rework. These requirements come in two flavors: functional (what the system must do) and non-functional (how well it performs). But here's the catch: this phase is the most challenging part of the SDLC. Getting requirements wrong creates a ripple effect that breaks the entire project. That's why you need to ask the right questions, listen to users, and document everything before you even touch a line of code.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 3 with SDLC phases and the question 'What are the requirements?'
Show slide 4 highlighting 'most challenging phase' and 'defined properly before building'
Show slide 6 with functional and non-functional requirements split
Show slide 16 with 'Requirement' and 'Functional/Nonfunctional' labels

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Proper requirements definition is the foundation of any successful project, ensuring everyone is aligned and avoiding costly mistakes down the line.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: Why is requirements definition considered the most challenging phase of SDLC?
quiz_choices: A) It requires advanced coding skills
B) It involves complex mathematical formulas
C) It's easy to misinterpret user needs

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your favorite apps hide dozens of steps behind simple clicks?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Functional requirements turn user goals into system actions. Take student enrollment—users want to sign up for classes, but the system needs detailed steps: search courses, view details, register, cancel. These processes become the system's functions. Developers map each requirement to specific actions, like logging in, selecting tabs, or clicking buttons. It's about turning vague needs into clear, actionable system features that match real user behavior.

visual_directions.description: Show Systems Development Life Cycle slide with Analysis phase highlighted
Display Slide 8 with SIS functional requirements
Show Slide 9 with enrollment process steps

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Functional requirements bridge user goals and system processes by detailing exact actions needed to achieve them
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What do functional requirements focus on?
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: How do systems turn user needs into features? 🧠 #FunctionalRequirements #SystemsDesign #SoftwareDevelopment #TechTips

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your phone's speed and reliability are part of its performance requirements?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Performance requirements are all about how well a system works. Think speed—like your phone responding under 2 seconds. Capacity, meaning how much data it can handle. Ease of use, so it feels intuitive. And reliability, so it works consistently. These indicators ensure your system doesn't just function, but functions smoothly. Whether it's a course search tool or a shopping app, performance requirements make sure users don't get frustrated waiting or crashing.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 10 with non-functional requirements overview
Highlight slide 12's performance indicators

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Performance requirements ensure systems are fast, scalable, user-friendly, and dependable.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: Which performance indicator measures how quickly a system responds to user actions?
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: 🚀 Performance Requirements: Why Your System's Speed & Reliability Matter #SoftwareDesign #TechTips

reel_scripts.hook: Did you know your data is at risk without proper security?
reel_scripts.narration_text: Security requirements are all the measures that keep your system's data safe from hackers and unauthorized users. Think about when you log into your account — your username and password are protected through encryption, which scrambles the data so only the right people can read it. Systems also use access controls to make sure only approved users can view sensitive info like medical records or financial data. Firewalls and virus protection add layers of defense, blocking malicious attacks before they can harm the system. Even something simple like requiring strong passwords helps prevent brute-force attacks. These measures work together to ensure your information stays private and your system stays secure.

visual_directions.description: Show slide 13: Security requirements examples
Show slide 10: Non-functional requirements overview
Show slide 12: Performance requirements slide for context

reel_scripts.key_takeaway: Security requirements ensure your data stays safe from hackers and unauthorized access.
reel_scripts.quiz_question: What's a key security measure for protecting user data?
reel_scripts.suggested_caption: 🔒 Protect Your Data! What Are Security Requirements? #TechTips #CyberSecurity #SystemDesign
